name: Tectonic Hazards
description: Earthquakes, volcanic eruptions and the structure of the Earth, including plate boundary processes and management
commonMisconceptions: Plates do not float on liquid rock - the mantle is solid but behaves plastically over geological timescales
The Richter scale is logarithmic, not linear - magnitude 6 is ten times greater than magnitude 5
Earthquakes cannot be predicted - only monitored. Prediction implies knowing when and where they will occur
Volcanic eruptions at constructive boundaries are typically less explosive than those at destructive boundaries
aqaRef: AQA 8035/1: The challenge of natural hazards - Tectonic hazards
edexcelRef: Edexcel 1GA0 Component 1 Topic 2: Weather Hazards and Climate Change (tectonic content via Global Hazards)
ocrRef: OCR B (J384): Our natural world - Tectonic hazards

name: Weather Hazards
description: Tropical storms, extreme weather events and UK weather hazards
commonMisconceptions: Tropical storms form over warm oceans (above 27 degrees Celsius), not over land - they weaken rapidly once they make landfall
The eye of a tropical storm is calm, not the most dangerous part - the eyewall has the strongest winds
Climate and weather are different - weather is day-to-day conditions, climate is the average over 30+ years
The UK does experience extreme weather events - students often underestimate UK weather hazards
aqaRef: AQA 8035/1: The challenge of natural hazards - Weather hazards
edexcelRef: Edexcel 1GA0 Component 1 Topic 2: Weather Hazards and Climate Change
ocrRef: OCR B (J384): Our natural world - Weather hazards

name: Climate Change
description: Evidence for climate change, natural and human causes, and strategies for mitigation and adaptation
commonMisconceptions: The greenhouse effect is not inherently bad - without it, Earth would be too cold to support life. The enhanced greenhouse effect is the problem
Climate change is not the same as the ozone hole - they are separate environmental issues with different causes
Natural climate change has occurred throughout Earth's history, but current warming is primarily driven by human activity
Mitigation means reducing causes, adaptation means adjusting to effects - students often confuse these terms
aqaRef: AQA 8035/1: The challenge of natural hazards - Climate change
edexcelRef: Edexcel 1GA0 Component 1 Topic 2: Weather Hazards and Climate Change
ocrRef: OCR B (J384): Our natural world - Climate change

name: Ecosystems
description: Small-scale ecosystems, global biomes and the balance between living and non-living components
commonMisconceptions: Energy is not recycled in an ecosystem - it flows through and is lost as heat at each trophic level. Only nutrients are recycled
A biome is defined by climate and vegetation, not by country borders
Removing one species from a food web can have cascading effects on the entire ecosystem, not just the next trophic level
Ecosystems are not static - they are dynamic and constantly changing in response to natural and human factors
edexcelRef: Edexcel 1GA0 Component 1 Topic 3: Ecosystems, Biodiversity and Management
ocrRef: OCR B (J384): Our natural world - Ecosystems

name: Tropical Rainforests
description: Characteristics, biodiversity, deforestation and sustainable management of tropical rainforests
commonMisconceptions: Rainforest soils are not fertile - nutrients are stored in the biomass, not the soil. Once trees are removed, soil quickly degrades
Deforestation does not just affect local areas - it has global consequences for climate and biodiversity
Tropical rainforests are not just found in South America - they also exist in Central Africa, Southeast Asia and Oceania
Sustainable management does not mean stopping all development - it means balancing economic needs with environmental protection
aqaRef: AQA 8035/1: The living world - Tropical rainforests
edexcelRef: Edexcel 1GA0 Component 1 Topic 3: Ecosystems, Biodiversity and Management (tropical rainforests)
ocrRef: OCR B (J384): Our natural world - Tropical rainforests

name: Hot Deserts & Cold Environments
description: Characteristics, opportunities, challenges and management of hot desert and cold environments
commonMisconceptions: Hot deserts are not always covered in sand - many are rocky or stony landscapes
Desertification is not the desert expanding outwards - it is the degradation of land on the margins due to human activity and climate
Cold environments are not lifeless - tundra supports diverse wildlife adapted to extreme conditions
Permafrost is not ice - it is permanently frozen ground that may contain soil, rock and ice
aqaRef: AQA 8035/1: The living world - Hot deserts / Cold environments
edexcelRef: Edexcel 1GA0 Component 1 Topic 3: Ecosystems, Biodiversity and Management (hot deserts and cold environments)
ocrRef: OCR B (J384): Our natural world - Arid and cold environments

name: Rivers
description: River processes, landforms, flooding and flood management strategies
commonMisconceptions: Rivers do not flow faster in mountains just because of gravity - velocity often increases downstream as friction decreases relative to volume
Meanders do not form because of obstacles - they form due to helicoidal flow within the channel
Oxbow lakes are not created by one flood event - they form gradually as a meander neck narrows over time
Hard engineering is not always better than soft engineering - it is more expensive and can cause problems downstream
aqaRef: AQA 8035/1: Physical landscapes in the UK - Rivers
edexcelRef: Edexcel 1GA0 Component 1 Topic 1: The Changing Landscapes of the UK (river landscapes)
ocrRef: OCR B (J384): Our natural world - Rivers

name: Coasts
description: Coastal processes, landforms, erosion, deposition and coastal management
commonMisconceptions: Constructive waves build up a beach and destructive waves erode it - students often get these the wrong way round
Longshore drift moves material along the coast, not out to sea
A stack does not form from a single wave - it takes thousands of years of erosion to form headland features
Managed retreat is not giving up - it is a deliberate strategy to allow natural processes where defences are not cost-effective
aqaRef: AQA 8035/1: Physical landscapes in the UK - Coastal landscapes
edexcelRef: Edexcel 1GA0 Component 1 Topic 1: The Changing Landscapes of the UK (coastal landscapes)
ocrRef: OCR B (J384): Our natural world - Coasts

name: Urban Issues
description: Urbanisation, challenges of urban growth in LICs and HICs, and sustainable urban living
commonMisconceptions: Urbanisation is not the same as urban growth - urbanisation is the increasing proportion of people living in urban areas
Squatter settlements are not just problems - they show community resilience and can be improved through self-help schemes
Not all migration is from rural to urban areas - counter-urbanisation and suburbanisation also occur
Brownfield sites are previously developed land, greenfield sites are undeveloped - students frequently mix these up
ocrRef: OCR B (J384): People and society - Urban futures

name: Economic Development
description: Measuring development, causes of the development gap, strategies for reducing it and case studies of NEEs

subskills.name: NEE Case Study (e.g. Nigeria or India)

commonMisconceptions: GNI per capita alone does not show how wealth is distributed - a country can have high GNI but extreme inequality
Aid is not always beneficial - it can create dependency and may not reach those who need it most
LICs are not poor because of a lack of resources - many have abundant natural resources but face barriers to development
The terms 'developed' and 'developing' are overly simplistic - the Brandt Line is outdated and countries develop at different rates
ocrRef: OCR B (J384): People and society - Economic development

name: Resource Management
description: Global distribution and consumption of food, water and energy resources, and strategies for sustainability
commonMisconceptions: Water scarcity is not just about rainfall - it is also about access, infrastructure and pollution
Renewable energy is not completely without environmental impact - wind farms and dams can still affect ecosystems
Food insecurity is not just about production - distribution, affordability and waste are also critical factors
The UK is not self-sufficient in energy - it imports a significant proportion of its energy needs
ocrRef: OCR B (J384): People and society - Resource management

name: Fieldwork & Geographical Skills
description: Fieldwork methodology, data collection, presentation and analysis, plus map skills and statistical techniques
commonMisconceptions: A six-figure grid reference gives a precise location, while a four-figure reference gives a grid square - students often confuse these
Contour lines close together mean a steep slope, not a high altitude
Correlation does not prove causation - a scatter graph showing a link between two variables does not mean one causes the other
Primary data is collected by the student themselves, secondary data comes from other sources - not all pre-existing data is secondary
ocrRef: OCR B (J384): Geographical skills and fieldwork
